entry.title: Re: ATC Execution in HANA 2025 does not show any results
entry.content:
To see why ATC results might not look like you expect, first check that the ATC run actually tried to check the right objects - go to "Monitor and Control Runs" in transaction ATC, find your ATC run there, and look at how many objects ("Total Entities") appear in the "Check Objects" phase. You can look at the exact list of objects checked via "Expert > Display Object Keys".

If there are no or only very few objects there and you are developing in a namespace, the most common pitfall is that the namespace is not set as a Producer namespace in your system. See Registering Name Spaces for how to enable ATC checks for objects in namespaces.

entry.title: Issue with DTW Import – Unexpected Ship To Creation and Change Log Behavior
entry.content:
Good morning,

we are experiencing an issue with one of our customers during the mass import of Business Partner data via DTW.

Specifically, in the DTW file we are only populating the following fields: CardCode and Global Location Number.

During the upload (DTW on table OCRD), SAP, for unknown reasons, automatically creates an empty Ship To address for some Business Partners — for example, 4 out of 15 test uploads — therefore inserting a record into table CRD1.

Furthermore, when checking the SAP change log, for the Business Partners where the Ship To address has been automatically created, the system reports — with today’s date (the DTW execution date) — all the previous changes that had already been recorded in the past.

In practice, SAP appears to “repeat” in the change history, under the current date, the latest modifications previously made, in addition to the actual changes generated by the DTW upload.

Have you ever encountered a similar issue? Do you have any insight into how this behavior could occur?

It is unusual that the system writes into different tables, even when DTW is not involved. Furthermore, there are no empty rows in the import file that could explain the automatic creation of a blank record.

 
Thanks
Chiara

entry.title: Re: "Net Price must be greater than 0 for all items." err...
entry.content:
Dear @R971,

Your situation is standard behavior in Public Cloud:

  • You cannot submit a supplier quotation if any item has net order price = 0 / blank. SAP states explicitly: The system doesn't accept supplier quotations with a net order price equal to 0

More details below:

https://learning.sap.com/courses/implementing-sap-s-4hana-cloud-public-edition-sourcing-and-procurement/carrying-out-request-for-price-solution-process-steps

  • Partial quotations are not supported in the Manage RFQs process and you cannot delete/remove the unquoted items from the supplier quotation also you can’t set item quantity to 0 to neutralize it

SAP Note below:

https://userapps.support.sap.com/sap/support/knowledge/en/3600086

So there’s no configuration fix in Public Cloud to allow submit with missing prices. Your 0.01 workaround is exactly what many teams do but as you said, it’s not business-acceptable

Clean ways to handle it (My proposal):

Option A: Create a subset RFQ for only the quoted items

1. In Manage RFQs and Copy your RFQ

2. Remove the items the supplier did not quote and keep only quoted items

3. Publish the RFQ you don’t necessarily need to send it again you can use it to record the response

4. Create/maintain the supplier quotation for this subset RFQ and Submit now every item has a real price so no blocking error

5. Optional add an attachment / note on the new RFQ or quotation referencing the original RFQ number + supplier quoted

So, you’ll have a different RFQ number for the recorded quote but you avoid invented prices

Option B: Raise enhancement request

SAP confirms this limitation and points customers to request an enhancement via SAP Customer Influence

SAP Note below:

https://me.sap.com/notes/2963059/E

Best Regards,

Dawid

entry.title: All that you love to know about SRMPROTOCOL
entry.content:
Please note that the term SRM used here stands for SAP Records Management.

Managing SAP database growth is a constant balancing act between system performance and compliance. If you’ve noticed the SRMPROTOCOL table climbing to the top of your "Top Growth" charts, you aren't alone. With some systems housing billions of records, understanding and managing this table is critical for a healthy SAP environment.

Here is a guide on what SRMPROTOCOL does, how to control it, and how to reclaim your space.

1. Understanding SRMPROTOCOL: The "Who, When, and What"

At its core, the SRMPROTOCOL table functions as the audit trail for SAP Records and Case Management. Whenever a case or record or document in SRM is created, modified, or deleted, the system generates an entry here to maintain a history of activity.

  • Audit Importance: This table is vital for legal and regulatory requirements. It ensures that businesses can track the lifecycle of a document or case for accountability.

  • Data Content: It stores the "SPS ID" (Service Provider Selection ID) and timestamps that link back to specific business objects.

Because these logs track every granular change, the table can grow exponentially in high-volume environments, eventually impacting database performance and backup times.

2. How to Control Log Growth

You don't have to let the table grow indefinitely. SAP provides built-in mechanisms to define how long these logs should live before they are eligible for removal.

Check Your Current Configuration

To see how your system is currently handled, follow these steps:

  1. Execute Transaction SRMREGEDIT.

  2. Locate the SPS ID relevant to your business (you can find these IDs within the SRMPROTOCOL table itself).

  3. Double-click the SPS Element ID.

  4. Navigate to the Classification tab to view the specific logging parameters.

Define "Log Keep Days"

Every organization has different audit requirements. SAP allows you to customize the expiry time for these logs. By setting a specific "retention period," you ensure that the system only flags entries for deletion once they are no longer legally or operationally required.

More details available in the Records Management Logging product documentation page.

3. Cleaning Up and Reducing Space

Once you have defined your retention policies, there are two primary methods to reduce the size of the SRMPROTOCOL table: Deletion or Archiving.

 

FeatureOption A: DeletionOption B: Archiving (Recommended for Compliance)
Primary ToolFM - SRM_PROTOCOL_DELETE_EXPIRY
SAP Note 795613

 If you are using Records Management as a standalone product, t-code ORGANIZER, consider referring to Archiving in Records Management to handle logs related to documents and records. Case Management: If you are using the framework of Case Management, t-code SCASE, consider referring to Archiving in Case Management for logs specifically tied to cases.

Data RetentionData is permanently removed from the system.

Data can be moved to long-term storage (external files).

Best Use CaseWhen logs are past their legal "Keep Days" and no longer needed.When logs must be kept for 5–10 years for audit/legal compliance.
System ImpactImmediate reduction in table row count; requires a "Reorg" to shrink disk files.High initial effort to set up, but provides a sustainable long-term solution.
Accessibility

Logs are deleted forever once the function module is processed.

Logs remain accessible via archive search tools if needed.

Handling Large Volumes

If you have a massive table, do not run the FM online. Instead, wrap the Function Module in a custom report and execute it as a background job to avoid timeouts and system strain.

Archiving is handled within the archiving framework. Each step is ideally scheduled as background job

entry.title: Re: Badi to maintain Attachment as mandatory at SES Level
entry.content:
Dear Customer,

Thank you for your query regarding making attachments mandatory at the Service Entry Sheet (SES) level.

We have reviewed the requirement in SAP S/4HANA Cloud (Public Edition). Currently, there is no released BAdI or standard configuration available that allows enforcing attachments as mandatory at the SES level during Save or Submit.

In the Public Cloud environment, only whitelisted enhancement options are permitted, and attachment validation logic is not exposed through extensibility.

As a possible workaround, you may consider implementing a workflow-based governance process, where approvers verify the presence of attachments and reject the SES if supporting documents are missing. Alternatively, if this requirement is compliance-driven, we recommend submitting an enhancement request via the SAP Customer Influence Portal for future consideration.

Please let us know if you would like guidance on setting up workflow controls.

Kind regards,
Aishwarya

entry.title: Re: Implementation and Customizing of Real-Time Instant P...
entry.content:
Hi all,

The solution that resolved the issue on our side is described below:

First, ensure that the V9 format is implemented in your system. If it is not yet available, please refer to SAP Note 2253571 – Collective information note: CGI_XML_CT, CGI_XML_DD extended with BAdI enhancement.

The following configuration steps were required:

  • DMEE: Update your pain.001.001.09 tree by adding the constant value “INST” in the node
    PmtInf > PmtTpInf > LclInstrm > Cd.

  • FBZP (Country level): Create the relevant payment method and assign the correct format.

  • FBZP (Company Code level): Ensure that the options
    “Single payment for marked item” and “Extended Individual Payment” are both activated.

  • OBPM4: Under Print Parameters, set the indicator “Print Immediately”.

After applying the above settings, the payment file was generated correctly.

I hope this information is helpful for others facing a similar issue.

entry.title: Re: Request for standard production reporting apps
entry.content:
Good day @nelybaf22 

Thank you for your question.

The Public Cloud provides alternatives to the classic list style reports like COOISPI you mentioned. Especially if your users want to see produced quantities and order progress in one place.

The public cloud also enables you to use role based Fiori monitor apps for production and embedded analytics view. You should focus on apps that explicitly supports process orders, not only production orders.

For your requirements I suggest:

You use the Monitor Production Orders or Process app to get a summarised view of your production and order progress, exception and progress monitoring without opening each order one by one. The Manage Production Order or Process Orders gives you the possibility to fix and manage orders that have issues based on the monitoring screen selection.

 

With the Monitor Work Center Schedules, you can focus on your work center with operational overview. You can visualise schedule and workload for each work center and it is useful for daily capacity and execution discussions.

See the following references for more details. https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_FIORI/5d441ea8c6ba4ee798d1a679165b3970/f8285e538f0e4510e10000000a441470.html https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_S4HANA_CLOUD/2dfa044a255f49e89a3050daf3c61c11/4e366afdf7604bc9b25f39b4aff05cb2.html?locale=en-US

If your users mainly want to know the weekly production, the most robust standalone source is usually material movement based reporting. The Public Cloud offers this function mainly via standard material document analytic apps and filters.

With the Monitor Material Coverage app, you can compare your produced quantity to your PIR. You can compare what was executed and MRP coverage. Therefore, providing you visibility to shortage and coverage. Basically, knowing what production is doing.

To summarise:

  • For your daily operations: use Monitor Production Order or Process Orders
  • For Production quantities: use Material posting based on analytics for actual produced quantity
  • For PIR comparison (optional): use Monitor Material Coverage to show you requirements side, then compare actuals.

This will give you a much more intuitive experience than a single complex list report.

Let me know if you have further questions.

Best regards

Chris

entry.title: Re: ConvertExternalUserOnStartDateJob behavior
entry.content:
Hi @angelosuano ,

You need to configure the 'ConvertExternalUserOnStartDateJob' to ensure that an external user is converted to an internal user on the employee’s start date. The start date will be honoured as long as the Time Zone is maintained and available in Job Information as part of the EC setup. If this job is not configured, the user’s state will remain ‘E’ (External), even after completing the MPH step.
 
Regards,
VR

entry.title: Clarification on SAP S/4HANA APIs for Customer, Billing, and Financial History Integration
entry.content:
Hi Experts,

I am currently working on integrating an external web application with SAP S/4HANA and would appreciate some guidance on selecting the appropriate APIs/services.

Application Context:
My application functions as a customer management portal. In the existing system (Oracle-based), we display and manage:

  • Customer data

  • Bill / Invoice details

  • Bill / Invoice history

  • Financial account (FA) history

  • To-Do management (Add / Update / Delete operations)

We now need to implement similar functionality using SAP S/4HANA.

Clarifications Needed:

  1. Customer Data
    Which APIs are recommended for retrieving and managing customer master data?

  2. Billing / Invoice Details
    Which standard APIs should be used to fetch:

    • Invoice / billing details

    • Invoice history / open items

  3. Financial Account / History
    For financial/account-related history (balances, transactions, etc.), which APIs or services are appropriate?

  4. CRUD Operations
    For operations such as Add / Update / Delete (e.g., To-Dos or related entities), should we rely on:

    • Standard OData APIs

    • CDS-based services

    • Custom APIs

  5. Best Practice Approach
    From an architectural perspective, what is the recommended approach:

    • Standard SAP APIs (API Hub)

    • Custom CDS Views + OData

    • RAP / BTP-based services

Any pointers to relevant APIs, documentation, or best practices would be greatly appreciated.

Thanks in advance for your support.

Best regards,
Kavinkumar

entry.title: Audit Logs Made Simple: Instant Access to the SAP Audit Log Viewer in SAP BTP
entry.content:
Audit logs in SAP BTP for the Cloud Foundry environment are essential for monitoring actions such as creating or updating subaccounts, changing users or roles, or performing actions that affect costs. Account administrators use them to review operational changes, troubleshoot problems, and maintain governance across landscapes. Until now, however, gaining insight into audit logs hasn't always been easy. Several configuration steps were required before you could access audit logs. 

Now managing audit logs in SAP BTP for the Cloud Foundry environment has become significantly more straightforward. Two major updates have been made available that eliminate manual setup and enable you to see audit logs across the entire account hierarchy. 

 

Access to the Audit Log Viewer on the Subaccount Level: Zero Configuration Required 

Previously, viewing audit logs meant working through a four-step process for each subaccount: adding entitlements, subscribing to the service, configuring role collections, and finally accessing the dashboard. That's now a thing of the past. 

The SAP Audit Log Viewer service is now automatically available for every subaccount. This applies to both new subaccounts and all your existing subaccounts. Simply click through from your Subaccount Overview page or use the Actions menu in the Account Explorer, and you're in.  

You no longer need to manually subscribe to the SAP Audit Log Viewer. The entitlement, subscription, and authorization setup happen automatically behind the scenes. Existing subscriptions to the SAP Audit Log Viewer service continue to work. But if you already have any automations or processes built around provisioning the Audit Log Viewer, we recommend that you update your scripts to skip the automatic provisioning of new subscriptions. 

 What this means: less administrative overhead, faster access to the information you need, and one less thing to remember when creating new subaccounts. 



Audit Logs for the Global Account: Finally Visible in the SAP BTP Cockpit  

The second update makes audit logs now accessible directly from the SAP BTP cockpit. Previously, accessing global account audit logs meant either submitting a support ticket or working with APIs. Now, it's as simple as clicking the Audit Log Viewer link in your global account's Actions menu.  

This straightforward access saves time and removes unnecessary barriers. The audit logs provide visibility into critical account-level activities, such as the creation or deletion of subaccounts, user and role assignments, destination management and budget management activities.  

 

These changes reflect a welcome shift toward reducing friction in day-to-day platform management and monitoring. Sometimes the best features are the ones that simply get out of your way.

entry.title: Understanding the Basic Terminologies of SAP Cloud World : A Beginner ‑ Friendly Guide
entry.content:
𝗗𝗲𝗺𝘆𝘀𝘁𝗶𝗳𝘆𝗶𝗻𝗴 𝗦/𝟰𝗛𝗔𝗡𝗔, 𝗕𝗧𝗣, 𝗥𝗔𝗣 & 𝗖𝗔𝗣, 𝗥𝗜𝗦𝗘 𝗪𝗜𝗧𝗛 𝗦𝗔𝗣, 𝗖𝗹𝗲𝗮𝗻 𝗖𝗼𝗿𝗲, 𝗖𝗙𝗟, 𝗦𝗶𝗱𝗲 𝗯𝘆 𝗦𝗶𝗱𝗲 𝗘𝘅𝘁𝗲𝗻𝘀𝗶𝗯𝗶𝗹𝗶𝘁𝘆 - 𝗔 𝗣𝗿𝗮𝗰𝘁𝗶𝗰𝗮𝗹 𝗝𝗼𝘂𝗿𝗻𝗲𝘆 𝗳𝗼𝗿 𝗔𝗕𝗔𝗣 𝗗𝗲𝘃𝗲𝗹𝗼𝗽𝗲𝗿𝘀
 
𝟭. 𝗦/𝟰𝗛𝗔𝗡𝗔 𝗗𝗲𝗽𝗹𝗼𝘆𝗺𝗲𝗻𝘁 𝗠𝗼𝗱𝗲𝗹𝘀 𝗘𝘅𝗽𝗹𝗮𝗶𝗻𝗲𝗱
 
𝗢𝗻-𝗣𝗿𝗲𝗺𝗶𝘀𝗲 
✔ You own/manage servers like ECC 
✔ Full ABAP Freedom - Custom Code, Debug, SE80/ADT
 
𝗣𝗿𝗶𝘃𝗮𝘁𝗲 𝗖𝗹𝗼𝘂𝗱
✔  SAP Manages Servers for you(𝗦𝗶𝗻𝗴𝗹𝗲 𝗧𝗲𝗻𝗮𝗻𝘁)
✔ Limited Custom Code - Must Follow Clean Core Principles
 
𝗣𝘂𝗯𝗹𝗶𝗰 𝗖𝗹𝗼𝘂𝗱
✔ Shared Infrastructure(𝗠𝘂𝗹𝘁𝗶 𝗧𝗲𝗻𝗮𝗻𝘁)
✔ No Custom ABAP - Extensions Via BTP only
 
𝟮. 𝗦𝗶𝗻𝗴𝗹𝗲 𝗧𝗲𝗻𝗮𝗻𝘁 𝗩𝘀 𝗠𝘂𝗹𝘁𝗶 𝗧𝗲𝗻𝗮𝗻𝘁 : 𝗧𝗵𝗲 𝗛𝗼𝘂𝘀𝗶𝗻𝗴 𝗔𝗻𝗮𝗹𝗼𝗴𝘆
 
𝗨𝗻𝗱𝗲𝗿𝘀𝘁𝗮𝗻𝗱𝗶𝗻𝗴 𝘁𝗵𝗲 𝗔𝗿𝗰𝗵𝗶𝘁𝗲𝗰𝘁𝘂𝗿𝗲 𝗗𝗶𝗳𝗳𝗲𝗿𝗲𝗻𝗰𝗲
 
■ 𝗣𝗿𝗶𝘃𝗮𝘁𝗲 𝗖𝗹𝗼𝘂𝗱( 𝗦𝗶𝗻𝗴𝗹𝗲 𝗧𝗲𝗻𝗮𝗻𝘁 )
 
𝗛𝗼𝘂𝘀𝗶𝗻𝗴 𝗔𝗻𝗮𝗹𝗼𝗴𝘆 : You Rent an entire independent house - SAP is your Landlord who Maintains it, but you're the only resident.
 
𝗪𝗵𝗮𝘁 𝘁𝗵𝗶𝘀 𝗠𝗲𝗮𝗻𝘀 :
✔  Your Data is Completely isolated from other companies
✔ You get your own dedicated hardware/software stack
✔ SAP manages infrastructure ( Servers, OS, backups )
✔ You still control configurations and some customizations
 
𝗘𝘅𝗮𝗺𝗽𝗹𝗲:
Company A :  Server 1 Running S/4 HANA Instance A
Company B :  Server 2 Running S/4 HANA Instance B
Company C : Server 3 Running S/4 HANA Instance c
 
Each Company has their own 𝗦𝗲𝗽𝗮𝗿𝗮𝘁𝗲 𝗶𝗻𝘀𝘁𝗮𝗹𝗹𝗮𝘁𝗶𝗼𝗻.
 
 
■  𝗣𝘂𝗯𝗹𝗶𝗰 𝗖𝗹𝗼𝘂𝗱 
 
𝗛𝗼𝘂𝘀𝗶𝗻𝗴 𝗔𝗻𝗮𝗹𝗼𝗴𝘆: You rent an apartment in a shared building - many families live in the same building, sharing elevators/parking but have separate locked units.
 
𝗪𝗵𝗮𝘁 𝗧𝗵𝗶𝘀 𝗠𝗲𝗮𝗻𝘀:
✔ Your data is logically separated (like database partitions)
✔ Same software version for all customers (no custom patches)
✔ Shared infrastructure (like AWS/Azure multi-tenancy)
✔ No custom ABAP allowed (everyone uses same codebase)
 
𝗘𝘅𝗮𝗺𝗽𝗹𝗲:
Single Server → Single S/4 Instance
  ├── Company A Data (Tenant ID: 001)
  ├── Company B Data (Tenant ID: 002)
  └── Company C Data (Tenant ID: 003)
 
𝗥𝗲𝗮𝗹-𝗪𝗼𝗿𝗹𝗱 𝗖𝗼𝗺𝗽𝗮𝗿𝗶𝘀𝗼𝗻
𝗣𝗿𝗶𝘃𝗮𝘁𝗲 𝗖𝗹𝗼𝘂𝗱 𝗶𝘀 𝗹𝗶𝗸𝗲:
Having your own ECC system but SAP hosts the servers
✔ You still get your own environment (DEV/QAS/PRD)
✔ Can have some customizations (within clean core limits)
 
𝗣𝘂𝗯𝗹𝗶𝗰 𝗖𝗹𝗼𝘂𝗱 𝗶𝘀 𝗹𝗶𝗸𝗲:
✔ Using Gmail - Google's servers, everyone uses same version
✔ You can't modify Gmail's code - only configure it
✔ Cost-effective but zero flexibility
 
𝗪𝗵𝗶𝗰𝗵 𝗖𝗼𝗺𝗽𝗮𝗻𝗶𝗲𝘀 𝘂𝘀𝗲 𝘄𝗵𝗮𝘁 ?
Manufacturing(Large) - Private Cloud/ On premise ( Heavy Customizations )
Retail(Small/mid) - Public Cloud ( Standard Process, Fast Deployments )
Start ups - Public cloud ( Cost Effective )
 
𝟯. 𝗪𝗵𝗮𝘁 𝗶𝘀 𝗥𝗜𝗦𝗘 𝘄𝗶𝘁𝗵 𝗦𝗔𝗣 ?
 
𝗥𝗜𝗦𝗘 𝗶𝘀 𝗡𝗢𝗧 𝗮 𝗽𝗿𝗼𝗱𝘂𝗰𝘁 - 𝗶𝘁'𝘀 𝗮 𝘀𝘂𝗯𝘀𝗰𝗿𝗶𝗽𝘁𝗶𝗼𝗻 𝗽𝗮𝗰𝗸𝗮𝗴𝗲 like buying a car with insurance/maintenance bundled.
 
𝗥𝗜𝗦𝗘 = 𝗦/𝟰𝗛𝗔𝗡𝗔 𝗖𝗹𝗼𝘂𝗱 (𝗣𝗿𝗶𝘃𝗮𝘁𝗲/𝗣𝘂𝗯𝗹𝗶𝗰) + 𝗕𝗧𝗣 + 𝗦𝘂𝗽𝗽𝗼𝗿𝘁 + 𝗠𝗶𝗴𝗿𝗮𝘁𝗶𝗼𝗻 𝗧𝗼𝗼𝗹𝘀
 
For you as a developer: It's just a licensing model. Your actual work doesn't change drastically unless your company chooses Public Cloud.
 
𝟰. 𝗪𝗵𝗮𝘁 𝗶𝘀 𝗕𝗧𝗣? 𝗧𝗵𝗲 𝗦𝗶𝗺𝗽𝗹𝗲 𝗧𝗿𝘂𝘁𝗵
 
𝗕𝗧𝗣 (𝗕𝘂𝘀𝗶𝗻𝗲𝘀𝘀 𝗧𝗲𝗰𝗵𝗻𝗼𝗹𝗼𝗴𝘆 𝗣𝗹𝗮𝘁𝗳𝗼𝗿𝗺) is SAP's cloud platform where you build apps that extend S/4HANA without touching its core code.
 
𝗢𝗹𝗱 𝗘𝗖𝗖 𝗪𝗮𝘆 (𝗬𝗼𝘂𝗿 𝗖𝘂𝗿𝗿𝗲𝗻𝘁 𝗪𝗼𝗿𝗹𝗱):
" Customer asks: "We need a custom approval workflow for purchase orders"
 
" 𝗬𝗼𝘂 𝗱𝗼 𝘁𝗵𝗶𝘀:
1. Create Z-table (SE11)
2. Write custom ABAP program (SE38/SE80)
3. Modify standard SAP transaction (User Exit/BADI)
4. Add custom fields to EKKO table
 
𝗥𝗲𝘀𝘂𝗹𝘁: System becomes "dirty" → upgrades break your code
 
𝗦/𝟰 + 𝗕𝗧𝗣 𝗪𝗮𝘆 (𝗡𝗲𝘄 𝗔𝗽𝗽𝗿𝗼𝗮𝗰𝗵):
You do this:
1. Keep S/4HANA standard (no modifications)
2. Build approval app on BTP (separate system)
3. Connect to S/4 via APIs
4. Store custom data in BTP database
 
𝗥𝗲𝘀𝘂𝗹𝘁: S/4 stays "clean" → smooth upgrades
 
𝟱. 𝗥𝗔𝗣 𝘃𝘀 𝗖𝗔𝗣
✔ 𝗥𝗔𝗣 = 𝗔𝗕𝗔𝗣-𝗯𝗮𝘀𝗲𝗱 (𝗿𝘂𝗻𝘀 𝗜𝗡 𝗦/𝟰𝗛𝗔𝗡𝗔)
 
• RAP runs inside S/4HANA system - whether Public or Private Cloud.
 
𝗥𝗔𝗣 𝗶𝗻 𝗣𝗿𝗶𝘃𝗮𝘁𝗲 𝗖𝗹𝗼𝘂𝗱 
 •  Create custom database tables (Z-tables)
•  Access standard SAP tables directly
•  Some custom ABAP classes (within clean core guidelines)
•  Traditional debugging in ADT

 

𝗥𝗔𝗣 𝗶𝗻 𝗣𝘂𝗯𝗹𝗶𝗰 𝗖𝗹𝗼𝘂𝗱
•  NO direct table access (VBAK, KNA1, etc.) - must use   released CDS views (I_* views)
•  NO custom database tables in S/4
•  Only released APIs/BAPIs allowed
•  Cannot use SE11/SE80 - only Eclipse ADT

 

✔ 𝗖𝗔𝗣 = 𝗡𝗼𝗱𝗲.𝗷𝘀/𝗝𝗮𝘃𝗮-𝗯𝗮𝘀𝗲𝗱 (𝗿𝘂𝗻𝘀 𝗢𝗡 𝗕𝗧𝗣 - 𝘀𝗲𝗽𝗮𝗿𝗮𝘁𝗲 𝗳𝗿𝗼𝗺 𝗦/𝟰)
 
• CAP is completely separate from S/4HANA - runs on BTP only.

𝗖𝗔𝗣 𝘄𝗶𝘁𝗵 𝗣𝗿𝗶𝘃𝗮𝘁𝗲 𝗖𝗹𝗼𝘂𝗱 𝗦/𝟰
𝗖𝗼𝗻𝗻𝗲𝗰𝘁𝗶𝘃𝗶𝘁𝘆:
• Uses SAP Cloud Connector (secure tunnel to on-premise/private)
• Can call any OData service you expose from S/4
• More flexible integration (can use custom RFCs if needed)

𝗖𝗔𝗣 𝘄𝗶𝘁𝗵 𝗣𝘂𝗯𝗹𝗶𝗰 𝗖𝗹𝗼𝘂𝗱 𝗦/𝟰
𝗥𝗲𝘀𝘁𝗿𝗶𝗰𝘁𝗶𝗼𝗻𝘀:
• Can only use SAP's released APIs (API Business Hub)
• No custom OData services from S/4 (since you can't create custom code there)
• Direct internet connectivity (no Cloud Connector)

 

𝟲. 𝗖𝗹𝗲𝗮𝗻 𝗖𝗼𝗿𝗲
 
𝗪𝗵𝗮𝘁 𝗶𝘁 𝗶𝘀: A principle that keeps your SAP system's standard code unchanged and upgrade-safe.
 
Simple explanation: Don't modify SAP's delivered code directly. Keep the "core" clean.
 
𝗘𝘅𝗮𝗺𝗽𝗹𝗲 :
𝗢𝗹𝗱 𝗔𝗽𝗽𝗿𝗼𝗮𝗰𝗵 :
 
Third Party System → Inbound IDoc → BADI/Customer Exit ( Data Mapping for Idoc segments ) → SAP Standard Processing
 
𝗖𝗹𝗲𝗮𝗻 𝗰𝗼𝗿𝗲 :
Third Party System → IDoc → AIF Layer → Data Mapping → SAP Standard Processing
 
 𝗕𝗲𝗻𝗲𝗳𝗶𝘁𝘀 𝗼𝗳 𝗔𝗜𝗙 𝗔𝗽𝗽𝗿𝗼𝗮𝗰𝗵:
✅𝗖𝗹𝗲𝗮𝗻 𝗖𝗼𝗿𝗲 𝗖𝗼𝗺𝗽𝗹𝗶𝗮𝗻𝗰𝗲:
 
•  No code in customer exits/BADIs
•  Configuration-driven transformations
•  Standard AIF framework handles processing
 
𝟳. 𝗕𝗧𝗣 𝗦𝗶𝗱𝗲-𝗯𝘆-𝗦𝗶𝗱𝗲 𝗘𝘅𝘁𝗲𝗻𝘀𝗶𝗯𝗶𝗹𝗶𝘁𝘆
 
𝗪𝗵𝗮𝘁 𝗶𝘁 𝗶𝘀: Building custom applications on SAP Business Technology Platform (BTP) that work alongside your SAP system.
 
𝗦𝗶𝗺𝗽𝗹𝗲 𝗲𝘅𝗽𝗹𝗮𝗻𝗮𝘁𝗶𝗼𝗻: Create new apps on the cloud that connect to SAP via APIs, without touching the core system.
 
𝗕𝗲𝗻𝗲𝗳𝗶𝘁𝘀: Innovation without risking core stability, use modern cloud technologies
 
𝟴. 𝗖𝗙𝗟 (𝗖𝘂𝘀𝘁𝗼𝗺 𝗙𝗶𝗲𝗹𝗱𝘀 & 𝗟𝗼𝗴𝗶𝗰)
 
𝗪𝗵𝗮𝘁 𝗶𝘁 𝗶𝘀: SAP's embedded extensibility framework for adding custom fields and business logic without code modifications.
 
𝗦𝗶𝗺𝗽𝗹𝗲 𝗲𝘅𝗽𝗹𝗮𝗻𝗮𝘁𝗶𝗼𝗻: Point-and-click tool to add fields and simple logic to standard SAP objects.
 
Add simple extensions inside SAP using key user tools.

entry.title: Blending in SAP Analytic Cloud Stories Part II: Filtering
entry.content:
<!doctype html>

 

Filtering Across Models in SAP Analytics Cloud Stories

Introduction
Filtering across models keeps multi-model stories consistent so users see the same subset of data when switching visuals or models. This article explains how to link dimensions for filter propagation, the difference between direct and indirect filters, step-by-step examples you can reproduce in the SAP Learning Hub practice systems, performance tips, and troubleshooting. 

Practice systems (recreate the examples)

  • Hands-on Practice for Exploring SAP Analytics Cloud — SACE11
  • Hands-on Practice for SAP Analytics Cloud: Story Design — SACS21
  • Hands-on Practice for SAP Analytics Cloud: Modeling and Data Transformation — SACM21
  • Planning practice systems SACP23 and SACP34 also work without restrictions

Models used in the examples

  • P00M_Pacifica_Order_Finance (Finance)
  • P00M_Pacifica_Shipping_Order (Shipping)

These are available under Public/SACE11/Content or Public/SACS21/Content in the practice systems.

Quick overview — how filtering across models works

  1. Link the common dimensions between models (Region, Store, Customer, Product, etc.). Linking makes the dimension behave as one logical dimension for propagation.
  2. Apply story/page/input control/widget filters. If the filter targets a linked dimension present in all target models, it is a direct filter and propagates immediately. If the filter targets a dimension not present in a target model, SAC applies an indirect approach: it filters the source model, extracts remaining linked-dimension members, then applies those members as filters on the other model(s).

Step 1 — Linking dimensions

Purpose: Create mappings so filter values propagate between models.

Step-by-step

  1. Open your story in edit mode.
    Screenshot_01_StoryEdit.png — Story open in edit mode
  2. Open the Data panel (Builder / Data area).
    Screenshot_02_DataPanel.png — Data panel visible
  3. Click Link Dimensions (or Manage Linked Dimensions).
    Screenshot_03_LinkDimensionsDialog.png — Link Dimensions dialog
  4. Select the two models to link (e.g., P00M_Pacifica_Order_Finance and P00M_Pacifica_Shipping_Order).
  5. Choose the common dimensions to link (Region, Store, Customer ID, Product) and the matching attribute (ID preferred for filtering; Description often used for display).
    Screenshot_04_SelectDimensions.png — Choose dimensions and attribute (ID/Description)
Tip: Link on ID for filter propagation when member names are not unique or descriptions contain blanks. Live HANA practice examples often require ID linking because descriptions may be null.

Step 2 — Apply filters and input controls

Once dimensions are linked, add story/page filters or input controls. Below are direct and indirect filter examples with expected behavior.

Direct filter example (linked dimension present across models)

Scenario: Region is linked between Finance and Shipping models. Applying a Region filter updates both models directly.

  1. Add a page filter or input control for Region.
    Screenshot_07_AddFilter.png — Add story/page filter
  2. Select Region values (e.g., USA, Canada) and apply.
    Screenshot_08_RegionFilterSet.png — Story filter set to USA, Canada
  3. Observe visuals from both models update to show only selected regions.
    Screenshot_09_DirectPropagation.png — Direct filter propagation

Behavior: Fast and predictable because the filter targets a linked dimension present in both models.

Indirect filter example (dimension not present in all models)

Scenario: Filter Product = “Boots & Hats” on Product Sales while Sales Managers model doesn’t include Product. SAC filters the Product Sales model first, extracts linked Region members that remain, then applies those Regions to Sales Managers.

  1. Add a filter or input control on Product in the Product Sales model.
    Screenshot_10_ProductFilter.png — Product filter added
  2. Select Product = “Boots & Hats”.
    Screenshot_11_ProductSelected.png — Product value selected
  3. Observe Sales Managers visuals update by the Regions associated with “Boots & Hats”.
    Screenshot_12_IndirectPropagation.png — Indirectly filtered Sales Managers view

Behavior: Indirect propagation can be slower because SAC computes matching members from the source model before applying them to targets. Performance depends on linked-dimension cardinality and count.

Filtering with hierarchies

When dimensions have hierarchies, consider level and descendant propagation:

  • Link the hierarchical dimension across models (prefer linking on ID rather than a specific hierarchy object when levels differ).
  • If you select a parent node, decide whether descendants should be included; where supported choose Include descendants or Exclude descendants.
Screenshot_13_HierarchyLink.png — Linking hierarchy and descendant options

Best practices for performance and correctness

  • Link dimensions whenever possible so filters can be direct and fast.
  • Prefer ID matching for filters — ID propagates reliably even with duplicate descriptions.
  • Minimize the number of linked dimensions when using indirect filters; each additional linked dimension increases processing complexity.
  • For large live datasets consider SDI-based blending/filtering (requires admin setup). Browser-based propagation is suitable for smaller aggregated datasets.
  • Validate link granularity — link at the most granular level required (Store vs Region) to avoid visualization warnings.

Troubleshooting common filter issues

  • Filters don’t propagate: check the target dimension is linked and the attribute (ID vs Description) matches actual values.
  • Visuals show warning icons or render errors: verify linked-dimension granularity meets visual requirements.
  • Ambiguous/duplicate members: switch to ID linking.
  • Slow updates with indirect filters: reduce linked-dimension count or test with smaller datasets; consider model integration.
  • Live data warnings about browser limits: follow the message and evaluate SDI if needed.

Interplay with blending

Filtering and blending use the same linked-dimension setup. When you blend visuals, linked dimensions determine how data is joined and how filters propagate. Blended visuals follow direct vs indirect filtering rules; heavy cross-model calculations often perform better if done at the model level.

Live data specifics

  • Live HANA examples often link on ID automatically when descriptions are null.
  • Browser-based propagation is default. For very large data volumes, use SDI-based blending/filtering to avoid browser limits and temporary aggregate persistence.
  • Practice systems use live HANA models — you may see warnings when linking live and imported models; these are expected.

Linking examples from practice systems

Note: Region in the practice models can have null descriptions and will link by ID automatically. When linking live & imported models SAC may warn about browser-based blending limits.

Step-by-step demo checklist

  1. Recreate linking steps for P00M_Pacifica_Order_Finance and P00M_Pacifica_Shipping_Order.
  2. Apply a direct filter on Region and confirm both models update.
  3. Apply an indirect filter on Product and confirm Sales Managers update by linked Region.
  4. Test hierarchy filtering and the exclude-descendants option.
  5. Note any performance or warning messages when linking live and imported models.

Resources and further learning

  • SAP Help Portal — Blending Data and Analysis across models (search “blending” / “filtering across models”)
  • SAP Community — Stories, Reporting, and Data Exploration forums
  • Analytics Training Videos and practice exercises in the SAP Learning Hub

Contact
Michael Melters — Education Senior Consultant, SAP
Michael.Melters@sap.com

Would you like me to generate the exact image filenames and short captions for each screenshot placeholder (so you can drop them into your CMS)?

entry.title: Re: Deep Dive into SAP Datasphere Object Store of BDC: Benefits, Architecture and implementation
entry.content:
@mdelibasic 

When you install a standard data product via intelligent applications, the data product tables are replicated to a SAP Managed HANA Disk space.  It makes replication flows, copying the data from a file based data product (foundation service) to a HANA Local Table in Datasphere for performance reasons.

First, Foundation Services (FOS) layer stores the "Bronze/Silver/Gold" versions of data products in HANA Data Lake Files (HDLF), but then the data must be surfaced in SAP Datasphere for modeling and consumption (HANA Disk and In-Memory). ref https://community.sap.com/t5/questions-about-sap-websites/install-sap-managed-data-products-in-bdc/qaq-p/14331376

 

No HDLFS is used when installing a standard data product, except for the foundation service of SAP that you cannot see.

When using Databricks, at this point you have to copy everything back to a HDLFS space if you want to enhance the data product in SAP Datasphere and share back with Databricks it seems 🤯.

entry.title: DMR - Quality Level
entry.content: I have two group counter for the same inspection plan: counter 1 is assigned to material X supplier A; counter 2 is assigned to the same material X but supplier B. Both have the same dynamic control rule attributed at the characteristics level. When I register GR from supplier A, inspection lot is correctly created with QLCH status and the quality level is created. When I register a GR from supplier B, the quality level is ignored and the inspection lot has no status in this regard. How is this possible? The flow is identical

entry.title: HUs disappearing from FO/FU after Goods Receipt in EWM .
entry.content:
Hello,


We have an outbound process integrated with TM based on Outbound Delivery.

  • Outbound Delivery → Freight Unit → Freight Order

  • Handling Units are created on the outbound delivery (no EWM in shipping plant)

  • HU are visible correctly on FU and FO

  • PGI is posted, events are reported in FO

  • An Inbound Delivery is automatically created in another plant (this plant uses EWM)

  • After goods receipt in EWM, the user in the shipping location reports TM events (Check-In, UnLoad End, Check-Out)

After Goods Receipt in the receiving EWM plant (and especially after reporting UnLoad End), all Handling Units disappear from the Freight Order/FU. TM then rebuilds the structure using Package Builder. 

Any idea how to stop it or why it happens? 

EDIT: What I noticed is that system changes Warehouse Execution Status for this relevant outbound delivery from blank (not relevant) to 4 (Delivery is ready for shippipng) once Unload Event is reported.

We dont use ASR for this location...

entry.title: Re: How to extend or disable password lifetime for SAP Da...
entry.content:
You can change the Password Policy Configuration by navigating to the datasphere menu: system -> security. Here, you have the option to set the password expiration time.
You can also disable the password policy for a specific database user. To do this, go to space management, select your space, and in the database access -> database users section, select your user (check the checkbox) and then click "edit privileges". At the very top, you will find the option to disable the password policy.

entry.title: SAP BTP ABAP Environment - Release 2602
entry.content:
SAP BTP ABAP Environment - Release 2602

 
On February 14, the new 2602 release was delivered as the successor to the 2511 release. In this blog post, we provide a detailed overview of the new features and enhancements included in this release, highlighting the key improvements and innovations introduced with 2602. Some highlights in the new release include the My Home as your new personalized entry page in SAP Fiori Launchpad, the CDS Embedded Analytics Star Schema Generator and the RAP recommendations for input assistance based on the user input and interaction history. 

The list of all new features including more information can be found on the What's New for SAP BTP ABAP environment page. The new AI-related features are listed separately on the What's New for ABAP Generative AI in ABAP Cloud.

ABAP Development Tools (ADT)

  • New Grouping Option Effect on transports in ATC Problems View
  • ABAP Testability: Enhancements to Test Double Frameworks (details)
  • Global User ID for ATC Exemptions
  • Displaying the Used Objects of CDS Service Definitions in the Relation Explorer

ABAP RESTful Application Programming Model (RAP)

  • Collaborative Draft,  now also works in cross-business object scenarios if both business objects are collaborative draft-enabled, and if the cross-business object scenario is fully draft scope-enabled (details)
  • Recommendations, previously, business users had to fill out input fields manually and choose the right values. The new RAP recommendations standardize and ease this process: In RAP applications on an SAP Fiori user interface, RAP recommendations provide input assistance by proposing field values for input fields based on the user input and interaction history. RAP recommendations can be implemented using deterministic rules or using generative AI, which predicts the most likely values for each input field based on the context of the data. By integrating the ABAP AI SDK powered by Intelligent Scenario Lifecycle Management, you can embed AI capabilities into the application, enriching traditional value help with context-aware suggestions (details)
  • Embedded Analytics Star Schema Generator, the Star Schema Generator Wizard streamlines the creation of star schema models using ABAP Core Data Services. It empowers you to build comprehensive analytical models with ease. This tool guides you through the entire generation process, ensuring a consistent and efficient workflow. The wizard supports the following key components: Transactional Scenario, Analytical Cube and Dimension Views (details)

Identity and Access Management

  • IAM App Assignment: So far, it was only possible to assign access to Fiori apps to business users indirectly via business catalogs. Now, it's also possible to directly create business roles based on IAM apps for SAP-delivered business catalogs
  • Maintain Business Roles, you can now directly assign IAM apps in the Maintain Business Roles app. This supports you in handling authorizations more flexibly. You can also use the Mass Change Wizard if you need to add or remove multiple IAM apps at once (details)
  • App References, the following apps now contain references to IAM apps: Maintain Business Roles, Manage Business Role, Changes After Upgrade, Display Restriction Types, Business Catalogs, Business Role Templates, IAM Information System (details)
  • Information System, the main entity Application is now deprecated. The successor will be the IAM app, which is now contained in the Main Entity dropdown list (details)
  • App Authorization Variant, you can now use the IAM app of the App Authorization Variant type. This gives you more flexibility in managing restrictions because it allows you to activate or deactivate app authorization variants to define different access rights per business role (details)

Manage API Snapshots

  • As a provider for released APIs, you can now download and upload API snapshots using the Manage API Snapshots app. This way, you can download API snapshots from the test system and upload them in the development system, thus preventing incompatible changes to your released objects early in the development process (details)

SAP Fiori Launchpad

  • My Home in SAP BTP ABAP environment 
  • Launching an App with a Transaction Code (details)
  • Download and Upload of Spaces 
  • Removal of Business Groups 

Landscape Portal

  • Publish Product: Publish up to Two Product Versions (details)
  • Deploy Product: System multi-select supported and performance improved (details)

Infrastructure

  • Optional Integration with SAP Key Management Service, SAP BTP ABAP environment provides an optional integration with the SAP Key Management Service (KMS). With this integration, it's possible to manage the SAP HANA Cloud database of an ABAP environment instance via SAP KMS (details)

entry.title: Re: Third Party Sales Order Processing in SAP S4HANA Clou...
entry.content:
Good day @SAPUser007_ 

Thank you for your question.

Your description highlights that you are mixing two different standard concepts and they behave differently in public cloud. Here is the reason:

  • Third party/bought in item in Sales Order creates a standard PR for external procurement from SD.
  • Subcontracting in MM procurement process where the PO item must be item category L and the system explodes components from BOM for the subcontractor. The cockpit only shows those true subcontracting PO and its component requirements.

That explains why your “PR from SO” works but your later “PO with account assignment L” does not drive subcontracting cockpit.

Scenario 1: The client creates a Sales Order. The items to be sold require rework (same material code) through a subcontractor. The client wants a Purchase Requisition (PR) to be created automatically when the Sales Order is created.

Clarification: In your attempt:

  1. You have changed the SO item to “Bought in” so the system created a PR. This is the standard third-party behaviour.
  2. You create a PO from that PR and set account assignment “L”.
  3. Components do not appear in subcontracting cockpit because:
    1. “Account assignment L” does not make a PO a subcontracting PO
    2. Subcontracting requires PO item category L plus BOM explosion and requirement dates, otherwise it will not show in the cockpit.

Suggestion, use true subcontracting procurement. This is a pure MM subcontracting flow. It is not PR automatically form sales order out of the box. You can achieve this by:

  • Creating a Subcontracting PO for the assembly material,
  • Maintaining or selecting the BOM based components,
  • Sending component to the subcontractor and post GR for the subcontracted assembly.

Here is a quick guide:

Use the Create Purchase Order Advanced app to create your PO with Item Category L

In your Subcontracting Cockpit app, filter as necessary and the PO appears with component requirements.

If your subcontracting PO still does not appear in the cockpit, then consult the following reference:

https://userapps.support.sap.com/sap/support/knowledge/en/3400834

https://userapps.support.sap.com/sap/support/knowledge/en/3311396

As a key point, this option gives you subcontracting cockpit visibility, but it is not automatically created from the SO.

A second option would be to treat it as external service rework instead of subcontracting. This is if your material code stays the same.

You mentioned “rework of the same material code”. Note that subcontracting is designed for finished components goods. If you are sending the same item for rework and receiving it back, you may consider modelling this as external service processing, not as subcontracting with component explosion.

Here is the value gain from that model:

  • Your Sales orders triggers purchasing for the service or depending on your process, purchasing is created from a production or maintenance order
  • You can track cost to the sales order item (or WBS) through account assignment but you do not use subcontracting cockpit.

You will not require forcing a subcontracting structure that does not match your business meaning.

 

Scenario 2: The client is selling air conditioners, but the installation will be handled by a third-party service provider.

This scenario describes a classic third-part sales pattern where the sales order has material item for the air conditioner and a separate service item for installation that is relevant to the third party, so a PR is automatically created at SO save.

See Configuring a Sales Order with a Third-Party Item and Sales Processing using Third-Party without Shipping Notification (BDK) for more details.

In your Create Sales Orders app, create SO with the various items (material and service item), ensure your service item is third party relevant and confirm in the SO item, then make sure PR exists for the installation item.

In Manage Purchase Requisition, open your PR created from SO, convert PR to PO to the installation vendor. Then confirm service and billing.

This is the recommended way for installation by third party.

To summarise:

  • Third party in sales automatically creates a PR but it is not subcontracting. See Carrying out Subcontracting Solution Process for more details.
  • Subcontracting cockpit only shows subcontracting PO items with component requirements.
  • For “rework of the same material” do not force subcontracting just to get a PR from SO.
  • For AC installation by a third-party, make use of a separate third-party service item in the sales order.

Let me know if you have further questions.

Best regards

Chris

entry.title: SAP PO to SAP Integration Suite Migration without migration tool (Basic Edition)
entry.content:
Introduction

Migrating from SAP PO to the SAP Integration Suite — especially when working with the Basic Edition — can be challenging due to the lack of official migration tool. However, with a structured and creative approach, it’s entirely feasible.

Here’s a quick summary of what I will cover in this blog.

  • Connection between Subaccount and SAP PO system
  • Objects extraction process
  • Designing approach of the integration flows
  • Configuration in SAP system for integrations
  • Challenges and key points to be consider and prechecks for this migration 
  • Configuration for File based (NFS) scenario.

Even without the full migration toolkit, with the right mindset and methodology, you can still carry out a successful and efficient PO-to-Integration Suite migration.

Project Background

Here I will be share the details from two of the migration projects where the landscape was like

Source SystemSAP PO 7.5 Single Stack
Target SystemSAP Integration Suite (Basic Edition)
Mapping TypesABAP Proxy, IDocs
Adapters UsedXI (3.0), HTTP, File (FTP), IDoc, REST, JDBC

 

System setup - Cloud Connector (Connection between Subaccount and SAP PO system )

Here we need to add SAP PO system as well along with the SAP system under the connected subaccount. This step is require to extract the objects required for the interface.

Steps: Select the correct subaccount in the SAP Cloud Connector and add one new system where the backend type is SAP Process Integration (from the dropdown) and prepare one connection (HTTP as protocol).

Subaccount --> Cloud to On prim connection --> add new system --> SAP Process Integration --> HTTP --> {SAP PO hostname/Ip and Port} --> {Virtual Host name and port}  --> uncheck Principle propagation --> Finish --> Connection Check

Under Resources --> add resource --> URL Path (/) --> Access Policy (All Sub paths)

Cloud Connector - System Configuration

 

 

 

In case of connection failure:

This can be happen and in maximum case this occur when your SAP PO system is not on the same network where Cloud Connector in configured. In such cases one SR need to be raise to SAP to whitelist the IP's or the SAP PO system.  --> Idle Approach

In some cases during landscape migration customer create tunnels between there on-prim application and the Rise in that case tunnel between SAP PO server and Rise need to be create. --> rare scenario

 

Objects extraction process

Before extraction process we need to add the SAP PO system in Cloud Integration as well (First step was to add PO system in Subaccount - done in above step).  

Navigate to Integration Suite --> Settings (Integrations) --> System --> add System (SAP Process Orchestration Systems)

Provide the details like System name, Version, server address and credentials. Just note here the server address will be the virtual hostname and port defined in the cloud connector.

 Once this is complete, inside the iflow objects can be imported from sap po to cloud integration. To do this open the integration flow (fresh interface i.e. with just message start and end) and go to Integration Flow tab (bottom) then References and required objects like message mapping, data/message types. service interface, wsdl etc.

Just by selecting the message mapping all the dependent objects like dt,mt,wsdl will be imported. In case the original interface in SAP PO is synchronous then only Operation mapping can be select and depended objects will be imported automatically, only when interface is Asynchronous then we need to select the message mapping.

Integration Suite - Objects import

 

After this import maximum work will be completed and only connection part are the one which need to be done.

With this the iflow designing for the migration interface can be completed but there are some additional Iflows need to be created for seamless processing. I will say those as common routing iflow which will be used to received the transaction from SAP to Integration Suite for Proxy & IDoc based scenario. 

We required these Iflow because whenever any transaction generate from SAP it will flow from one destination only to the SAP PO to replicate the same we need to change that destination from SAP PO to Integration suite and for that we need the endpoints. To achieve this we create 3 common IFlows. 1 IFlow to receive all the IDocs from SAP, 2nd IFlow to receive all the ASynchronous XI message and 3rd IFlow to receive all the Synchronous XI message.

Key points while importing Messgae Mapping/Operation Mapping in Iflow design.

  • In case of Async interface only messgae mapping import can be done.
  • In case of Sync interface Operation mapping can be import and it will automatically import all depended objects (Message Mappings for both request and response) along with the wsdls (messgae structure).

Once everything is imported just placing them in at the right place is the manual thing.

Common routing flow configuration

Create 3 flow with the configuration mentioned below.

Iflow 1/2: Receive Sync/Async interface from SAP

Sync/Async Iflow Configuration

 

 

 

Here for the sender adapter XI exposed the endpoint eg "/proxy/async" and "/proxy/sync" for async and sync interfaces from SAP and to route these call to the correct interface mentioned "/${header.SapInterfaceName}" in the Address in process direct adapter.

When a call is generated from SAP, in the API call some header are created where one of the header is "SapInterfaceName" is the one which we can use as this contain the name of the service interface which is implemented at ABAP side. With the same actual SI name we can expose the actual interface which has all the message mapping transfomation etc.

 

Iflow 3: Receiver IDoc from SAP

Here based on the messgae type routing those to perticuler interface can be done and can put mulitple conditions based on the payload data. 

 

 

SAP Configuration for Sync/Async call

There are some configurations in SXMB_ADM which need to do for both Sync and ASync interface.

Step 1 - Create 3 destinations in SM59 with Type G "HTTP Connection to External Server" 

  • 1st destination for SAP to Integration suite Async Interface (SAP_TO_IS_PROXY_ASYNC)
  • 2nd destination for SAP to Integration suite Sync Interface (SAP_TO_IS_PROXY_SYNC)
  • 3rd destination for SAP to Integration suite IDoc send (SAP_TO_IS_IDOC)

Step 2 - Decide which type of interface need the defualt configurations. I recommended chosse which has less number of interface. In my case ASync interfaces are less in number so all default configuration will be for Async. 

Step 3 - configure Sender/Reciver ID. 

SXMB_MONI --> UNDER CONFIGURATION (CONFIGURE SENDER/RECEIVER ID)

Here create a entry with below data. similar way entries we have to create for all the interface which need additional configuration.

Step 4 part 1 - setting up the IDoc destination

go to WE21 - Create XML port (SAP_TO_IS) and maintain the destination created in step 1 "SAP_TO_IS_IDOC" and assign this XML HTTP port to requried partner profile in WE20.

Content type : application/x-sap.idoc

HTTP version: 1.0

SOAP protocol : checked

Step 4 part 2 - setting up the 

Under SXMB_ADM --> Integration Engine Configuration

At initial page the destiantion maintained should be the default destination. In my case that destination belongs to the ASync interface. 

Role of Business System : LOC Application Ssystem

Associated Integration Server : dest://SAP_TO_IS_PROXY_ASYNC

Then go to configuration and maintain the rules for Sync interfaces (Sender/Receiver ID) which are created under Step 3.

 

This is all what we need to do to migrate SAP PO running interfaces to SAP CPI without using Migrtion tool in case you have the basic edition.

 

For information : I did this migration 1 year back so might be there are some steps which i might used in old ways but this is working absoulty fine till now. I appriate the feedback if community has some.

 

Thanks 

Aman Sharma

entry.title: Re: Form country/region can't be initial in Record defect...
entry.content:
Go to BRF+ Transaction --> Click on Workbench --> Open Object --> Provide the object id as '40F2E9AFC5011EEAA9B392C6926BAF0A' & click on search.After that Expand Expression --> Decision Table --> Double click on DT_FORM_TEMPLATE --> Remove the Sender Country & Activate the changeThen Expand Function --> Double Click on FU_FORM_TEMPLATE --> Go to Code Generation --> Click on Generate Function & generate it for With & Without Trace --> Save it
To solve the issue

entry.title: Re: Standard usecases of Service Order in SAP DM
entry.content:
Hi,

Service Orders were introduced to bridge PM Service Orders and DM for execution to repair / rework parts (SFCs) produced once produced in DM, sold to customers, that are now coming back to be “serviced” in some fashion with the main objective to use the same DM functionalities like the POD, data collection, nonconformance, etc.  Often, the same operators and manufacturing centers are used in serving the equipment returned that was used in the original manufacturing.

Check SNote 3379404 - SAP Digital Manufacturing: Restrictions and Limitations Note for details on potential restrictions and limitations.

Hope it helps! Br, Manoel

entry.title: Re: How to resolve atc issues
entry.content: About CHECKMAN: "Transaction is not released for public use". It's only used internally by SAP.

entry.title: Re: SAP DMC Production Order split
entry.content:
Hi,

In this case, you will need different SFCs for proper traceability. You can evaluate using 'Relaxed Flow' flag to release different quantities into different SFCs, see How 'Relaxed Flow' flag in Manage Recipes/Routings app affects Order Release and SFC quantities , or you can evaluate releasing a single SFC and then splitting it. Once you have multiple SFCs, you can process each one of them in the planned time frame, and all traceability will follow properly.

Hope it helps! Br, Manoel

entry.title: Re: Bring data from SAP Datasphere to Snowflake

entry.title: How to show Ramp-Up Coverage in SAP
entry.content:
Hello,

I am reaching out because I would like to understand how it is possible to manage a ramp-up plan in SAP.

Our company is working with Product View planning (RRP3).
The issue is that our current ramp-up plan is completely unachievable, and the realistic plan should be reduced — however, management does not allow us to lower the demand from a business perspective.

Could someone please explain how we can present to management our stock coverage based on existing contracts and Purchase Orders, while keeping the demand realistic in SAP?

Thank you in advance for your support.

entry.title: Re: SAP S/4HANA Migration Cockpit – Migrate Data Directly from SAP System – Using Staging Tables
entry.content:
Hey there,

imagine data is missing or incorrect (like payment terms or duplicate addresses), you can adjust them in the staging tables accordingly. You can use a database management system of your choice (e.g. DBeaver).

Please be aware that you are in responsibility for data consistency. 

Best Regards

Philipp

entry.title: SAP Commerce Cloud + Composable Storefront (Spartacus) multi-site with data isolation
entry.content:
Hi SAP Community,

I’m working on an SAP Commerce Cloud implementation with SAP Composable Storefront (Spartacus) in a multi-site setup.

I have configured my sites with data isolation = true.

Issue

During login via OAuth (token endpoint), authentication works only if I use a “decorated” user ID in the following format:

email|baseSiteUid

For example, in Postman the call to /authorizationserver/oauth/token (password grant) works if I send username=email|site, but it fails if I send only username=email. From a UX perspective, the user should enter only the email address, so I would expect SAP Commerce to resolve the baseSite automatically and decorate the UID internally.

Questions

What is the correct configuration in SAP Commerce / Composable Storefront to make the login request site-aware so that login works with only the email address?

Are there any best practices or official examples for Composable Storefront + data isolation (multi-site) regarding login handling?

Thanks in advance for any guidance!

Kind regards,

Stefania Zanzottera

entry.title: Sales price condition in Customer Projects for Subcontractor
entry.content:
Hello

There is an issue with Subcontractor (contingent worker) Sales price condition for customer project.

If we maintain sales price 100 € per hour using personal number and WBS ID this condition is not appearing in planned revenue.

We used then this access sequence to maintain price 100 € and this condition calculated project planned revenue.

After this subcontracting PO has been created for 80€ per hour and timesheet enterted. But revenue is recognised 80€ from PO price not the 100€ sales price from project.

Now i created same test and maintained project sales rate 100€ per hour using access sequence project and personal number

PO is same 80€ per hour and this time timesheet entry recognized revenue from project sales price:

I am not able to understand the reason why revenue reconisition is correct only when sales price access sequence project and personal number. Please help.

entry.title: Group Valuation in CO-PA SAP S/4HANA Private Cloud
entry.content:
Dear SAP-Community,

With regards to valuation views, the three following views exist: Legal Valuation View, Group Valuation View and Profitcenter Valuation View. We would like to display the group valuation in CO-PA within the SAP S/4HANA Private Cloud.

In detail, our target is to display the contribution margin based on group manufacturing costs. Preferably, we would like to use Margin Analysis and not cost-based CO-PA (no value fields). Our planned approach was to activate scope item 5W2 (own ledger 4G).

Due to contradicting articles, we are now uncertain whether this is possible or not. In case it is not possible – are there any workarounds to analyse contribution margin based on group valuation?

Help is highly appreciated, thanks in advance.

Kind regards,

Jakob
